nativeRange.habitat: Found on rocky and coral reef slopes, typically at depths of 18 to 120 m. This species tends to be collected from deeper water than most anthias commonly seen in the aquarium trade.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
refugium with copepods and amphipods recommended

diet.notes: Generally feeds on zooplankton in the wild. In captivity has been found to accept frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, and high-quality marine flake or pellet foods. A refugium producing copepods and amphipods is commonly recommended to supplement feedings, particularly during the acclimation period.

avoidWith: fish that outcompete for food at high speed

imageCandidates.notes: Victor, Teitelbaum & Randall (2020); 1880x1195. Striking, tack-sharp in-situ reef photo of a live, vibrant pink-red individual clearly showing the diagnostic red saddle blotches along the back and yellow body spots. Highest resolution and most diagnostic.
Izuzuki; 640x480. Sharp, whole-fish side profile of a live individual with yellow body and orange-red saddle blotches on the back; correct ID, different angle for variety.
Izuzuki; 640x480. Sharp, colorful live individual with green-tinged dorsal, red caudal-base and faint dorsal saddle spots; adds variety of coloration/sex.

summary: The Red Saddled Anthias is a deep-water shoaling species from the Indo-West Pacific, generally considered advanced to keep due to its sensitivity during acclimation. It tends to do best in small groups with ample live-rock hiding places, open swimming space, and supplemental zooplankton feeding.
careNotes:
Red Saddled Anthias tend to be collected from depths of around 30 m (roughly 98 feet), so newly acquired specimens are often sensitive to shipping stress and are commonly advised to be introduced to low-to-moderate lighting initially. Acclimation has been reported as challenging; keepers generally recommend introducing this species before more active or territorial tankmates are added. A refugium producing copepods and amphipods is widely suggested to ease the transition to prepared foods.

This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — a form of sequential sex change in which all individuals begin life as females, and the largest female in a group will transition to male if the dominant male is lost. Keeping a small shoal (generally 3 or more individuals) with one male and multiple females is considered more natural and tends to reduce stress-related losses.

Keepers in cooler-running systems (24–25°C) have kept this species successfully, and current data suggests it can tolerate the warmer temperatures typical of tropical reefs as well.

Note on scientific name: This species was formerly placed in the genus Pseudanthias but has since been reclassified to Pyronotanthias. Both names continue to appear in the aquarium trade, and husbandry resources are commonly listed under the older name Pseudanthias flavoguttatus.
breedingNotes: The Red Saddled Anthias has not been established as a captive-bred species, and no reliable breeding protocol has been documented in the hobbyist literature. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, all individuals begin life as females, with the dominant fish in a group transitioning to male when no male is present — meaning sex ratios within a shoal are dynamic over time. Spawning in the wild involves broadcast release of eggs and sperm into open water.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (10.2 cm male, 8.2 cm female), temperature range (24.2–28.3°C), native distribution, and taxonomy. FishBase lists the current accepted name as Pyronotanthias flavoguttatus. Provides wild-capture temperature data used to average against LiveAquaria's hobby recommendation.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding of this species has not been established, and no reliable protocol has been documented in the hobbyist literature.
